content.problemStatement:
A sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ of length NNN is called good if any one of the following hold:


The sequence is strictly increasing, or
The sequence is strictly decreasing, or
There exists an index i  (1<i<N)i\;(1 \lt i \lt N)i(1<i<N) such that:

Aj<Aj+1A_j \lt A_{j+1}Aj​<Aj+1​, for each 1≤j<i1 \le j \lt i1≤j<i
Aj>Aj+1A_j \gt A_{j+1}Aj​>Aj+1​, for each i≤j<Ni \le j \lt Ni≤j<N


For example, the sequences [3,5,9][3, 5, 9][3,5,9], [2,1][2, 1][2,1], [5,7,4,1][5, 7, 4, 1][5,7,4,1] are good, while [6,7,7][6, 7, 7][6,7,7], [5,2,3][5, 2, 3][5,2,3] are not.

You are given an array AAA consisting of NNN integers. Find a good sequence by rearranging the elements of the array AAA or report that this is impossible. If there are multiple good sequences, print the **lexicographically largest** one.

If two sequences AAA and BBB have the same length NNN, we say that AAA is lexicographically larger than BBB if there exists an index iii, (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) such that A1=B1,A2=B2,…,Ai−1=Bi−1A_1 = B_1, A_2 = B_2, \ldots, A_{i-1} = B_{i-1}A1​=B1​,A2​=B2​,…,Ai−1​=Bi−1​ and Ai>BiA_i \gt B_iAi​>Bi​.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN, denoting the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, denoting the given array.


Output Format
For each test case, if it is not possible to rearrange the given array into a good sequence, print −1-1−1. Otherwise, print a single line containing NNN space-separated integers - the lexicographically largest good sequence that can be made.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line. If it is impossible to rearrange the given numbers into a good sequence, print -1. Otherwise print N space‑separated integers – the lexicographically largest good sequence that can be formed.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: all numbers are distinct, descending order 5 3 2 is a strictly decreasing (good) sequence and is lexicographically largest.
Test case 2: array contains duplicates (three 1s), impossible.
Test case 3: after sorting descending we get 7 5 2 2 1, but there is a duplicate 2, so we cannot use decreasing order. However we can arrange 2 7 5 2 1 which is increasing then decreasing and uses each value once; it is the lexicographically largest good sequence.
Test case 4: duplicates make any good sequence impossible, hence -1.

solution.approach:
A good sequence must be strictly monotone (increasing or decreasing) or strictly increase up to a peak and then strictly decrease. In any of these cases every adjacent pair must be different, therefore the whole sequence must contain distinct values. If the multiset contains a duplicate, no good sequence exists. If all values are distinct, the lexicographically largest good sequence is obtained by sorting the numbers in descending order – this yields a strictly decreasing sequence, which is a valid good sequence and starts with the maximum possible element, then the next maximum, and so on. The algorithm for each test case:
1. Read N and the array.
2. Sort the array in non‑increasing order.
3. Scan the sorted array; if any adjacent elements are equal, output -1.
4. Otherwise output the sorted array.
The sorting dominates the complexity.
Correctness proof sketch:
- Lemma 1: Any good sequence must have all elements pairwise distinct. Proof: each adjacent pair must satisfy a strict inequality, so equality is impossible anywhere.
- Lemma 2: If the input contains a duplicate, no good sequence exists. Directly from Lemma 1.
- Lemma 3: For a set of distinct numbers, the descending order is a good sequence (strictly decreasing). Hence a solution exists.
- Lemma 4: Among all good sequences built from the same distinct set, the descending order is lexicographically maximal. Proof: The first element of any good sequence cannot exceed the maximum element of the set; descending order uses that maximum. Conditioning on the first element being maximal, the second element cannot exceed the second‑largest element, which descending order also uses, and so on by induction.
Thus the algorithm returns -1 exactly when impossible, otherwise the required lexicographically largest sequence.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case due to sorting; total O((ΣN) log N_max) ≤ O(5·10^5 log 10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array per test case.
